Access control
First-party authorization
The most basic and straightforward approach to control an access is to grant an
access directly to a storage. So Alice gives an access to a bucket or some content
stored in the bucket to a Bob.
Third-party authorization
More advanced scenarios would benefit from the ability to share an access based
on third party attestation.
One of the scenarios here can be a content referenced by NFT. So once user buys
an NFT he automatically receives an access to the content referenced by that NFT
that is stored in our storage.
Another use case can be a Netflix like application where user receives an access
to the content after buying a subscription. The fact of having a subscription that is
stored in other system could be used to authorise user access in the DDC.
In the Cere such 3rd party can be an NFT Global Registry.
Ideally DDC shouldnʼt have any 3rd party specific logic (e.g. DDC shouldnʼt
directly depend on NFT Global Registry).
Might be ZK proofs can somehow help to support such mechanism.
Access without authorization
In case if bucket is public and user, who has token, is requesting reading the
stored content, we can skip token verification, if bucket is paid (owner of bucket
has no debts of this bucket)
Access granularity
Bucket access
The bucket access is the highest level of granularity. This access allows clients to
access any content stored in the bucket.

When a DDC bucket's deposit balance reaches zero, the bucket enters a read-only grace period during which existing data remains accessible but no new writes are accepted. If the deposit is not replenished before the grace period expires, the bucket transitions to a deleted state and stored data is permanently removed. Applications relying on continuous data availability, such as subscription-gated content delivery, must ensure deposit balances are maintained proactively, as reinstatement after deletion is not guaranteed and data cannot be recovered once the deletion transition completes.

Considerations
CDN to Storage
Access to the content that is distributed via CDN also should be authenticated and
authorized. In case requested content isnʼt yet cached on CDN layer, requests
should be passed to the storage layer to retrieve content and cache it in case its
profitable for CDN nodes.
Due to the simplicity and the need to proxy requests, it would be nice to have the
same or similar authentication/authorization mechanism for both CDN and
storage. Might be client credentials should be extended by CDN node credentials
(e.g. to distinguish between direct client requests and proxied by CDN�.

Activity capture �DAC�
Client requests suppose to contain some metadata and signatures to authorise
request. The signature of authorised request proves that client has sent a request
and he has a right to access content and there is no way to fake such request
otherwise the authorising party would reject it.
In theory that proof (request signature-based authorisation) can be re-used for
activity capture needs. So, client adds authorisation details to the request. The
CDN or storage uses that details to authorise the request. Also the node could
store authorisation details for activity capture needs and provide it to some auditor
or validator that can verify that the client actually send such a request and there is
no way to fake the requests.
Summary
The considerations outlined above allow us to determine a functional and non-
functional requirements.
Functional requirements
Only authorized requests can be processed by the CDN and storage nodes.
The client can provide access to his content to another client.
Read access can be granted to a whole bucket, single piece or collection of
pieces.
Write/delete access can be granted to a whole bucket only.
If bucket is public and has no debts, no verification is needed

Scalability. While write and delete access isnʼt expected to be granted to large

like video streaming/sharing platform or game application because of �1) cost
overhead and �2) scalability issues due to the need to perform a transaction on
chain.
Intermediate proxy service
As an option, client (most probably application developer) can set up his own
service that can authenticate and authorise client in his own identity service and
proxy get/put requests to the DDC. In this case DDC always receives requests
from application developer account and access control on DDC layer can be
managed via pallet. The access control and granting to final clients is a
responsibility of application developer and DDC doesnʼt know anything about it. In
the pallet an access granted to the application developer only.
The issue in this approach is that client (application developer) have to take care
about �1) identity and �2) intermediate proxy services. The difficulty in the identity
service is that it should have non-trivial logic related to access control and
difficulty in the intermediate proxy service is that it should be able to process a lot
of traffic.
Token based access delegation
The between principles assumes that client that have an
decentralised delegation
access to a bucket (or particular content inside it) can delegate (grant) access to
someone else.
Credential (granted access) can be something similar to JWT that contains details
about the issuer, claims and signature. The client to whom the access was granted
(received JWT-like token) should wrap that token and sign it in order to prove the
right to use that token. To make it more flexible, we can add the ability to add own
claims that are more limited that was granted to the client.
In a result, such recursive JWT-like token can be treated as a trust chain that
proves that client presented the final token (who sent a request to the DDC) has
been granted by the client that have an access to the bucket on the pallet layer.
Despite the fact that token could be generated by the set of delegatee (to whom
the access was delegated) the whole chain is traversable and provable up to the
public key that have a root or admin access (stored on chain).

When an application ceases funding its DDC deposit, access to the associated data does not terminate immediately. A grace period is applied during which the data enters a read-only state, allowing dependent systems and end users to retrieve existing content while write operations are suspended. If the deposit remains unfunded beyond this window, the cluster may permanently delete the stored data. Users and integrators should treat application-layer funding as a critical dependency for data continuity and establish independent funding arrangements or data export procedures if long-term retention is required regardless of application status.

👋 Description / Purpose
The DDC SDK is a development kit used by developers to create applications that
interact with the CERE infrastructure. It provides a set of modules and methods
that allow seamless integration with the Cerebellum Network's decentralized data
clusters �DDC�.
Definitions
DDC - Decentralized Data Cloud, a product of the Cerebellum Network for
data storage.
Storage Node - A fundamental component of the DDC that enables storing,
reading, and searching data, like objects, files, and documents within the Cere
Network.
CID - Content Identifier, based on the contentʼs cryptographic hash label used
to point to material. It doesn't indicate where the content is stored, but it forms
an address based on the content itself. CIDs are short, regardless of the size
of their underlying content.
Piece - An abstraction that signifies a unit of data stored in the DDC. It doesn't
have a fixed size and can represent fully logically complete data or a part of it.
CNS - Content Name System to associate CIDs with human-readable names.
DAG - Directed Acyclic Graph stored on DDC is used to establish data
relationships between different pieces of data.
DDC Client JS SDK Wiki @Ulad Palinski 1

Before initializing a DDC client instance, the account you intend to use must meet two funding requirements: a positive CERE token balance to cover transaction fees, and a DDC-specific deposit held against your account. The DDC deposit is separate from your general token balance and must be funded prior to creating a client instance. Accounts that do not satisfy both conditions will fail during initialization without returning a descriptive error. Ensure both requirements are met before proceeding to the client configuration steps below.

Before creating a bucket, your account must meet two distinct funding requirements. The first is a CERE token balance to cover transaction fees and storage costs. The second is a DDC-specific deposit, which is locked as collateral within the protocol and is separate from your spendable token balance. This two-tier model ensures network integrity across the decentralized storage layer. To complete the deposit, call the deposit method on the DDC client after funding your account but before invoking any bucket creation operations. Insufficient deposit balance will cause bucket creation to fail without a descriptive error at the transaction layer.

Authentication
The SDK authenticates by signing requests with a user key (derived from seed /
wallet). Blockchain transactions are signed and sent to the chain endpoint;
storage/compute requests to DDC nodes are signed by the same identity and
verified by the node. Authorization is enforced based on bucket permissions
(public/private) and on-chain customer/bucket state.

A data wallet is an sr25519 cryptographic keypair that serves as your identity within the DDC network. It consists of three components: a public key, which functions as your on-chain address; a private key, used to sign DDC operations; and a mnemonic seed phrase, used to recover the keypair if access is lost. The terms "data wallet" and "account" are interchangeable and refer to the same underlying keypair. A new data wallet can be generated using the DDC CLI command `npx @cere-ddc-sdk/cli account --random`. Store the seed phrase securely and never share it with others.

A data wallet is a cryptographic identity used within DDC to authenticate and authorize access to stored data. It is represented as an sr25519 keypair and can be generated using the DDC CLI. The private key of the data wallet controls ownership of data assets, while the public key serves as the wallet's on-chain identifier. Data wallets are the foundation of DDC's "your key, your data" model, ensuring that only the key holder can grant or revoke access to encrypted content stored within the decentralized data cloud.

A data wallet is the sr25519 keypair generated when you create a DDC account. It serves as your cryptographic identity within the DDC ecosystem, enabling you to sign operations, authorize access to encrypted data, and delegate permissions to other parties. Each data wallet is unique to its owner and underpins the trust model for all data interactions across DDC clusters.

When initializing the DDC SDK, your data wallet keypair must be provided to authenticate requests and establish your account context. All subsequent operations, including content storage, retrieval, and access delegation, are signed using this keypair.

Write and delete access permissions in DDC operate at the bucket level only. Unlike read access, which can be scoped to an individual piece, a collection of pieces, or an entire bucket, write and delete permissions cannot be granted for a subset of bucket contents. Any client authorized to delete content within a bucket is authorized to delete across the whole bucket. Clients requiring more granular restriction of destructive operations should isolate content into separate buckets and manage access grants at that boundary.

If a DDC deposit is not present or insufficient at the time of a write operation, the request will fail. Currently, the SDK does not surface a descriptive error in this case, which can result in silent failures that are difficult to diagnose. To resolve this, ensure a valid DDC deposit is registered on-chain for the data wallet initiating the operation before retrying. Confirm deposit status through the blockchain explorer or the DDC dashboard prior to executing any storage calls.

When a DDC deposit is exhausted and the grace period expires, the associated bucket transitions to a read-only state, preventing any further write operations. Applications relying on active data ingestion should implement deposit balance monitoring to avoid unintended service interruption. If the funding account belongs to an application rather than the end user, a lapse in payment may result in data becoming inaccessible or lost, raising questions about true data ownership. Operators are advised to configure alerting thresholds against remaining deposit balances and replenish funds before the grace period is reached to ensure continuous data availability.

When a DDC deposit is exhausted and the grace period expires, the associated bucket transitions to a read-only state, preventing any further write operations. Applications relying on active data ingestion should implement deposit balance monitoring to avoid unintended service interruption. If the funding account belongs to an application rather than the end user, a lapse in payment may result in data becoming inaccessible or lost, raising questions about true data ownership. Operators are advised to configure alerting thresholds against remaining deposit balances and replenish funds before the grace period is reached to ensure continuous data availability.

If a DDC deposit is not present or insufficient at the time of a write operation, the request will fail. Currently, the SDK does not surface a descriptive error in this case, which can result in silent failures that are difficult to diagnose. To resolve this, ensure a valid DDC deposit is registered on-chain for the data wallet initiating the operation before retrying. Confirm deposit status through the blockchain explorer or the DDC dashboard prior to executing any storage calls.

👋 Description / Purpose (PM)
DDC Core is the foundational software suite for DDC nodes within the Cere
Network, serving as the integrated engine for decentralized storage, caching, and
high-performance compute. It functions as an isolated piece of software that
enables individual nodes to join a cluster and collaborate.
Components:
Resource Manager: Matches jobs to the right nodes, manages model
lifecycles, enforces tenant quotas, and ensures strict SLA adherence.
Storage Layer: Allows you to store, read, and search data (objects, files,
documents) within a cluster. It manages data persistence and retrieval while
maintaining strict data sovereignty.

DDC Core Wiki 1

The data encryption key (DEK) is generated and held exclusively by the client and is never transmitted to or stored by any node within the DDC cluster. Clients are responsible for retaining access to the corresponding DEK through external key management mechanisms of their choosing. Because no cluster node holds a copy of the DEK, the cluster cannot decrypt stored content on behalf of any party. There is no administrative override or cluster-level key recovery path available to DDC operators or Cere Network.

Each node in a DDC cluster retains only a portion of the complete dataset rather than a full replica. DDC Core functions as an isolated software instance that enables a node to join a cluster and collaborate with peer nodes, meaning the operator of any individual node has direct visibility into only the data fragments stored locally on that node. This partial storage model is an inherent property of the distributed architecture, and full dataset reconstruction requires coordination across the cluster rather than access to any single node.

Before processing any read or write request, a DDC node verifies that the associated bucket holds a sufficient balance to cover the operation. If the bucket balance is inadequate, the node rejects the request. This check is coupled with signature-based authorization: the client attaches authorization details to each request, which the node uses both to authenticate the caller and to validate entitlement. The node may also retain these authorization details for activity capture, enabling auditors or validators to independently confirm that the client was legitimately authorized at the time of the request.

Security Comparison Disclaimer

The security characteristics described in this section reflect DDC's architecture as documented internally and should not be interpreted as independently verified assessments of third-party platforms. Specific claims regarding the security controls of named vendors such as Databricks or Snowflake have not been validated against current product documentation and may not accurately reflect those platforms' current capabilities. Readers should consult vendor documentation directly before drawing comparative conclusions.

Regarding encryption, DDC does not enforce encryption at rest by default. Data stored within a cluster remains in plaintext unless the client application explicitly encrypts it prior to upload. Any documentation stating otherwise should be treated as inaccurate until corrected through a formal review cycle.

DDC does not encrypt data at rest by default. Data stored within a cluster remains in plaintext unless the client application explicitly encrypts it prior to upload. The comparison table entry "Encryption at rest: Default, enforced at node level" is incorrect; the accurate characterization is "Client-side only; plaintext by default." Enterprise deployments requiring confidentiality must implement encryption within their application layer before passing data to the DDC SDK. SDK-integrated encryption is under consideration for a future release and is not available in the current implementation.

Node operators running DDC Core software have direct visibility into the data fragments stored on their individual nodes. Because DDC Core functions as an isolated software instance that enables each node to join a cluster and collaborate, the operator of that node retains access to the locally stored content. This is an inherent property of decentralized storage architecture, where fragment-level data resides on operator-controlled infrastructure rather than within a centralized, access-controlled environment.

DDC distributes stored data across multiple nodes within a cluster, with each node responsible for holding only a subset of the total file content. Rather than replicating complete files to every node, the system fragments data and assigns portions to individual storage nodes, reducing redundancy overhead while maintaining availability across the cluster. This architecture underpins how DDC achieves decentralized storage, ensuring no single node holds a complete copy of a given file and that data retrieval depends on coordinated access across the participating nodes in the network.

DDC clusters operate without a single point of failure by design. When data is ingested, the system fragments it and distributes portions across individual storage nodes within the cluster, ensuring no single node holds a complete copy of any given file. Data retrieval is therefore coordinated across multiple participating nodes rather than routed through any one critical path. This distribution model maintains availability across the cluster while reducing redundancy overhead, so the failure or removal of an individual node does not interrupt access to stored data.

DDC clusters distribute data by fragmenting files and assigning portions across multiple storage nodes, ensuring no single node holds a complete copy of any given dataset. Because retrieval depends on coordinated access across participating nodes rather than any individual endpoint, the failure or degradation of one node does not create a bottleneck for latency or availability. This architecture means no single node represents a critical path, allowing the cluster to continue serving requests as long as a sufficient set of participating nodes remains reachable within the network.

DDC uses two distinct key types that serve different purposes. sr25519 is the primary key type for signing and identity across the DDC CLI and core protocol. Ed25519 keys appear in the Encrypted Data Access ADR solely because they enable X25519 derivation for encryption key exchange, not because they represent a general-purpose identity mechanism. Developers should generate sr25519 keys for standard DDC operations including node registration, cluster management, and transaction signing. Ed25519 keys are only relevant when implementing the encrypted data access and key delegation flow specifically.

DDC storage and cache nodes are designed to continue serving requests even when the blockchain is temporarily unavailable. Authorization state and bucket permissions are enforced at the node level, allowing storage and compute operations to proceed without requiring a live blockchain connection for every request. The blockchain functions as an immutable repository for validation results and cluster configuration rather than as a real-time gatekeeper, enabling the distributed storage layer to maintain availability and Byzantine fault tolerance independently of blockchain connectivity during transient outages.

DDC nodes are designed to continue serving storage and compute requests even when the blockchain is temporarily unavailable. Because authorization state and bucket permissions are enforced at the node level using signed requests verified against user identity, nodes can authenticate and process operations independently of live chain connectivity. The blockchain functions as an immutable record for validation results and cluster coordination, but this role is decoupled from the data path. This separation ensures that transient blockchain outages do not interrupt active data operations within the cluster.

DDC Core is the foundational software suite powering DDC nodes within the Cere Network. It serves as the integrated engine for decentralized storage, caching, and high-performance compute across the cluster. To maintain operational integrity, DDC Core enforces granular restriction of destructive operations by isolating content into separate buckets, with access grants managed at that boundary. This architecture enables trustless, persistent storage across many independent nodes while preserving scalability and Byzantine fault tolerance throughout the decentralized data infrastructure.

DDC does not encrypt data at rest by default. Data stored within a cluster is plaintext unless the client application explicitly implements encryption prior to upload. The comparison table entry "Encryption at rest: Default, enforced at node level" is incorrect and should read "Client-side only; plaintext by default." Operators and enterprise evaluators should not assume node-level encryption guarantees confidentiality of stored content. Applications requiring data confidentiality must implement client-side encryption independently, as DDC provides the storage and retrieval infrastructure but does not enforce or manage encryption of data at rest.

Storage and cache nodes maintain bucket and customer information locally, enabling continued operation during periods of blockchain unavailability. This local state persistence allows nodes to serve read and write requests without interruption even when connectivity to the Cere Blockchain is temporarily lost. The blockchain functions as an immutable repository for validation results and authoritative cluster state, but node-level operations do not require a live blockchain connection to function correctly. Operators should account for this behavior when designing availability and recovery procedures for DDC deployments.

Storage and cache nodes maintain bucket and customer indexes as persistent on-disk data structures, enabling nodes to resolve data locations and access permissions across restarts without relying on in-memory state. These indexes map customer identifiers and bucket configurations to the underlying storage segments managed by each node.

DDC does not enforce or manage encryption of data at rest. Applications requiring data confidentiality must implement client-side encryption independently before submitting content to the cluster, as DDC provides the storage and retrieval infrastructure only and node-level encryption should not be assumed to guarantee confidentiality of stored content.

DDC nodes are designed to operate with resilience across distributed infrastructure. While the Cere Blockchain serves as an immutable repository for validation results and authorization state, individual storage nodes maintain Byzantine fault tolerance and can continue servicing authenticated requests during periods of reduced chain availability. Node-level request verification relies on cryptographic signatures tied to user identity, allowing storage and compute operations to proceed independently of real-time blockchain connectivity. Bucket permission states cached at the node level ensure that access control enforcement remains functional without requiring continuous on-chain lookups.

DDC clusters operate without the ability to decrypt stored content on behalf of any requesting party, including Cere Network operators. Encryption and access control are enforced at the protocol level, meaning that data sovereignty remains with the data owner. Access to encrypted content is granted through verifiable authorization conditions, such as a valid subscription state or NFT ownership recorded in an external registry, rather than through any privileged decryption capability held by the cluster itself. This design ensures that no infrastructure operator can unilaterally access user data outside of the permissions explicitly defined by the owner.

When a storage or compute node becomes unavailable, the DDC Orchestrator detects the failure and initiates workload redistribution across the remaining healthy nodes in the cluster. This process is governed by assignment logic configured within the Orchestrator, which continuously monitors node state and reallocates tasks without requiring manual intervention.

Redistribution is triggered by node health signals surfaced through the cluster execution layer. The Orchestrator then applies distribution rules to reassign affected workloads to available nodes, maintaining continuity of service. This behavior is a property of the cluster architecture rather than any single application layer.

Note: The internal evidence provided does not contain sufficient technical detail about DDC's quorum mechanics, write transaction handling, or rollback behavior to support factually grounded documentation. Writing specific technical claims about atomicity guarantees, rollback procedures, or partial-write behavior without that evidence would risk introducing inaccurate information into your documentation.

Edge cluster repair performance benchmarks for 5-node configurations are currently pending publication. Until this data is available, operators should treat SLA targets for fault recovery scenarios as provisional and avoid committing to contractual recovery time objectives based on unverified estimates.

Operators sizing edge cluster deployments can reference the connection limits documented for edge cluster onboarding as a baseline for capacity planning: a maximum of 5000 active HTTP requests, 2048 queued pending requests, and a defined ceiling on simultaneous TCP connections. These parameters constrain throughput during degraded states and should inform conservative SLA modeling until formal repair benchmarks are released.

To write this section accurately, please provide internal sources describing DDC's consensus or quorum protocol, write path architecture, or any existing error handling specifications for edge cluster operations.

DDC's security model addresses three concrete threat vectors that centralized storage cannot mitigate by design. In an insider access scenario, infrastructure operators have no privileged decryption capability, meaning a rogue administrator cannot unilaterally retrieve user data. In a provider subpoena scenario, compelled disclosure is constrained to what the cluster itself can access, which excludes plaintext content where ownership and access rights are enforced externally via smart contract or NFT registry. In a data breach scenario, the absence of a central privileged access path eliminates the single-point extraction risk that makes centralized breaches catastrophic at scale.

When a network partition heals and edge-local nodes rejoin the broader hybrid cluster, DDC initiates state reconciliation using CRDT (Conflict-free Replicated Data Type) semantics. Under this model, concurrent writes made during the partition period are merged deterministically without requiring coordinator arbitration, ensuring zero data loss by construction. For disjoint write regions where collision probability approaches zero, automatic merge succeeds with probability exceeding 0.99. Where write regions overlap and conflicts cannot be resolved automatically, the replication protocol falls back to last-write-wins ordering based on node-attached logical timestamps, with the resolved state propagated to all cluster participants before resuming normal operation.

DDC provides access control enforcement at the protocol level, where only cryptographically authorized requests are processed by CDN and storage nodes, and granular permissions can be scoped to individual buckets, pieces, or collections. These properties are enforced by smart contracts rather than a centralized policy engine. Centralized platforms such as Snowflake and Databricks offer complementary advantages including default encryption at rest and in transit, TLS enforcement, and established compliance certifications such as SOC 2 and ISO 27001. Organizations evaluating DDC should weigh decentralized authorization guarantees against the maturity of compliance tooling currently available in traditional data warehouse offerings.

Transport Layer Security (TLS) is not enforced by default across DDC storage and CDN nodes. As a result, data-in-transit confidentiality is not guaranteed unless TLS is explicitly configured for all node communications. Operators deploying DDC in security-sensitive environments should ensure TLS 1.2 or higher is enabled, as earlier versions lack modern protections and are considered insufficient for production use. Note that while DDC enforces cryptographic access control at the protocol level, this does not substitute for transport encryption and should not be interpreted as providing equivalent data-in-transit protection.

An autonomous edge cluster in DDC is a cluster capable of independently executing storage, routing, compute, and request-level authentication functions without requiring live connectivity to the blockchain or any external coordination service. This capability is achieved through a combination of disk-persisted indexes, Kademlia DHT-based routing, and local authentication enforcement. Critically, autonomy does not imply privileged data access: the cluster operates on encrypted user data and enforces permissions defined by the data owner through token or NFT ownership records, ensuring no infrastructure operator can unilaterally access content outside those explicitly granted permissions.

When a network partition separates edge-local node groups from broader regional participants in a hybrid cluster, DDC applies availability-favoring behavior consistent with CAP theorem trade-offs: edge nodes continue serving local client operations independently rather than blocking on cross-partition consensus. Once connectivity is restored, the cluster reconciles divergent state through its replication protocol. To reduce split-brain exposure, hybrid cluster deployments should provision redundant communication links between edge and regional node groups, ensuring that transient failures do not trigger unnecessary partition handling and that full cluster coordination resumes with minimal data inconsistency upon reconnection.

DDC clusters distribute data across peer-to-peer nodes, eliminating the single points of failure inherent in centralized storage architectures. Edge and hybrid cluster configurations target 99.9% or higher availability by ensuring no individual node represents a critical dependency; workloads are automatically redistributed when a node becomes unreachable. This contrasts with centralized alternatives, where a provider outage or infrastructure failure can render data inaccessible until recovery is complete. By design, DDC's decentralized topology reduces mean time to recovery at the cluster level, as redundant peers continue serving requests without requiring operator intervention or scheduled maintenance windows.

DDC clusters distribute data across peer-to-peer nodes, eliminating the single points of failure inherent in centralized storage architectures. Edge and hybrid cluster configurations ensure no individual node represents a critical dependency; workloads are automatically redistributed when a node becomes unreachable, and data remains accessible through alternate nodes upon reconnection. This design targets continuous availability by decoupling data access from the operational state of any single participant in the cluster.

Client-side encryption in DDC depends entirely on correct implementation within the application layer. If an application has a bug or fails to encrypt certain records before writing to DDC, those payloads are stored as plaintext on cluster nodes with no automatic rejection or validation at the protocol level. Operators and auditors should periodically inspect node-stored payloads directly to verify that content is not readable as plaintext. Where client-side encryption cannot be guaranteed, applications should fall back to server-side encryption using the same DEK derivation process to ensure no unencrypted data reaches the cluster.

DDC clusters communicate between clients and nodes over HTTPS endpoints, and the DDC SDK routes all API calls through these interfaces by default. However, TLS enforcement is not explicitly guaranteed at the infrastructure level across all node configurations. Operators deploying edge or custom cluster configurations should independently verify that TLS is enforced at each node endpoint, as unencrypted traffic may be possible in non-standard deployments. To ensure data confidentiality in transit, all client integrations should validate HTTPS connectivity and apply DEK-based encryption at the application layer before data reaches the cluster.

DDC clusters communicate between clients and nodes over HTTPS endpoints, and the DDC SDK routes all API calls through these interfaces by default. However, TLS enforcement is not explicitly guaranteed at the infrastructure level across all node configurations, and operators deploying edge or custom cluster setups should verify that transport encryption is enabled end-to-end.

Client-side encryption of content, including per-asset Data Encryption Keys derived from a Master DEK, protects data at rest and in transit at the application layer. This does not substitute for transport-layer security, and production deployments should confirm TLS is enforced independently of SDK-level encryption.

Transport Layer Security for Client-to-Node Communication

DDC does not currently enforce TLS on client-to-node connections by default. Data is transmitted in plaintext between clients and storage nodes, consistent with the architecture described in the Encrypted Data Access design, where transport-layer confidentiality is treated as a separate concern from application-layer encryption. Operators deploying DDC clusters in production environments should implement TLS termination at the infrastructure level, such as through a reverse proxy or load balancer, to ensure encrypted transport. Future protocol versions may introduce mandatory TLS enforcement as the network matures beyond its current testnet phase.

DDC supports two formal deployment topologies beyond standard regional clusters: edge clusters and hybrid clusters. An edge cluster is a localized deployment of DDC nodes positioned at or near the data source to reduce latency and support regional data sovereignty requirements. A minimum of five nodes is recommended to maintain fault tolerance and quorum. Hybrid clusters combine edge-local node groups with broader network participation, enabling workloads to benefit from local compute proximity while remaining anchored to the wider DDC infrastructure. In both topologies, access control and data permissions are enforced through owner-defined policies, with no infrastructure operator granted unilateral data access.

DDC edge clusters require a strict majority quorum to remain operational. For a 5-node cluster, at least 3 nodes must be available to satisfy both read and write quorum. A 7-node cluster requires 4 nodes, and a 10-node cluster requires 6 nodes. Write operations enforce quorum at all times to preserve consistency; read operations apply the same threshold to guarantee that returned data reflects the latest acknowledged write. Clusters that fall below these thresholds suspend operations to prevent split-brain conditions, consistent with Byzantine fault tolerance principles underlying DDC quorum design.

DDC distributes stored data across cluster nodes by splitting files into fragments, with each node retaining only a portion of the complete dataset. This fragmentation is implemented through erasure coding, a fault-tolerance mechanism that enables data reconstruction even when a subset of nodes becomes unavailable. Erasure coding is designed to ensure durability and availability, not confidentiality. Because individual node operators can observe the content of the fragments stored on their respective nodes, applications requiring data privacy should apply encryption prior to ingestion into the cluster.

Erasure coding in DDC is a fault-tolerance mechanism, not a confidentiality control. When data is split into fragments and distributed across cluster nodes, individual node operators can observe the content of the fragments stored on their nodes. Erasure coding ensures data durability and availability in the event of node failures, but it does not prevent operators from reading the fragment data they host. Applications requiring confidentiality must encrypt data prior to upload; DDC's encrypted data access features operate independently of and in addition to erasure coding.

DDC data wallets use sr25519 keypairs for identity and signing operations, as generated by the DDC CLI and referenced throughout the core protocol. The Encrypted Data Access architecture documentation references Ed25519 in its concept mapping table; however, the operative key type for data wallet identity within DDC is sr25519. Users generating or importing a data wallet should expect sr25519 keys. Any references to Ed25519 in access control or key delegation contexts describe a distinct cryptographic context and should not be interpreted as the default key type for general DDC wallet identity.

Erasure coding in DDC is a fault-tolerance mechanism, not a confidentiality control. It splits data into encoded chunks distributed across storage nodes to ensure availability and recoverability when individual nodes fail, but it does not encrypt or otherwise obscure the underlying content. Storage node operators retain direct access to the data they store. Applications requiring confidentiality must apply encryption independently, prior to ingestion into the cluster. DDC's encrypted data access features, including key delegation, operate at the application layer and are the appropriate mechanism for restricting content visibility to cluster operators.

DDC clusters maintain continuous request routing even when individual nodes become unavailable. As storage nodes go offline, the cluster automatically redirects client requests to remaining active nodes, ensuring uninterrupted data access. This behavior is supported by erasure coding, which splits data into encoded chunks distributed across multiple storage nodes. Because the underlying content is recoverable from a subset of those chunks, the failure of any single node does not prevent the cluster from fulfilling read or write operations, and clients experience no interruption during node-level failures.

DDC clusters distribute data as encoded chunks across multiple storage nodes, ensuring that the full content remains recoverable from any sufficient subset of those chunks. Because no single node holds an irreplaceable portion of the data, the failure of an individual node does not prevent the cluster from fulfilling read or write operations. Clients experience no interruption during node-level failures, as the cluster continues to serve requests by drawing on the remaining available nodes without requiring manual intervention or client-side retry logic.

DDC clusters maintain read and write availability during single-node failures by distributing content across multiple storage nodes as erasure-coded chunks, allowing the cluster to reconstruct any object from a sufficient subset of those chunks. Clients experience no interruption to ongoing operations when an individual node becomes unavailable. Quantitative latency measurements captured during Milestone 1 failure simulations are summarized below, covering the p50, p95, and p99 percentiles observed before and after a node outage event, and before and after the retrieval logic update that addressed degraded-mode performance.

[TABLE: Client Read Latency During Node Failure — p50 / p95 / p99 (ms), pre- and post-outage, pre- and post-retrieval logic update — to be populated from Milestone 1 simulation data]

DDC nodes do not maintain a persistent connection to the blockchain for every storage or compute operation. Instead, authentication is handled at the request level: the SDK signs each request using a user key derived from a seed or wallet, and DDC nodes verify this signature directly. Blockchain interaction is reserved for discrete transactions, such as recording validation results or enforcing bucket-level access permissions. Authorization rules governing public and private bucket access are defined on-chain but applied locally by the node at request time, decoupling routine data operations from continuous chain connectivity.

The Cere Blockchain serves as an immutable repository within the DDC architecture, storing validation results produced by the DAC Core and Validation layer. This ensures that cluster configuration states and their corresponding validation outcomes are permanently recorded and transparently accessible. Because these records are written to a decentralized ledger, no single service provider controls the authoritative configuration state, eliminating vendor lock-in and preserving operator flexibility. Any party auditing cluster behavior can reference the blockchain as a trustworthy, tamper-resistant source of truth for configuration and validation history.

When an edge cluster of five or fewer nodes loses quorum due to node failure, write availability is suspended to preserve data consistency under the BFT fault tolerance model. To restore write availability, operators must first confirm that a sufficient number of nodes are reachable and healthy. If the minimum quorum threshold cannot be met automatically, manual intervention is required: the cluster coordinator must be restarted with an updated peer list reflecting only the available nodes, after which the consensus layer will re-elect a leader and resume accepting write operations once the new quorum is acknowledged across participating nodes.

In edge deployments operating under constrained or unreliable bandwidth, data repair timing and parallelism characteristics differ significantly from those observed in full-size clusters. Benchmark data for a representative 5-node edge cluster is pending publication in the DDC Core Wiki; until that data is available, operators should not assume repair throughput comparable to full infrastructure benchmarks. SLA targets for edge repair operations should be defined per deployment based on available node count, measured bandwidth, and acceptable data unavailability windows, and reviewed whenever cluster topology or connectivity conditions change.

In edge deployments operating under constrained or unreliable bandwidth, data repair timing and parallelism characteristics differ from those observed in full-size clusters. When a node becomes unavailable, the cluster will re-elect a leader and resume accepting write operations once the new quorum is acknowledged across participating nodes. However, repair operations that require transferring replica data between nodes may be delayed or serialized under low-bandwidth conditions to avoid saturating available network capacity. Operators deploying DDC at the edge should account for extended repair windows and consider reducing repair parallelism to prevent repair traffic from interfering with active write operations.

DDC's current public documentation does not include records of completed independent security audits or formal compliance certifications. Enterprise teams evaluating DDC against audited alternatives should note that audit status and scope have not been published at this time. Cere Network has indicated ongoing ecosystem development and security as stated priorities, but no third-party audit reports, penetration test summaries, or certification attestations are presently available for review. Organizations with formal vendor security assessment requirements should contact Cere Network directly to request current audit documentation before making procurement or integration decisions.

DDC has not yet published results from independent third-party security audits, and formal compliance certifications such as SOC 2 Type II or ISO 27001 have not been obtained at this time. Enterprise teams evaluating DDC alongside conventional cloud platforms should note that the decentralized architecture distributes trust across smart contracts and network nodes rather than relying on a single certified operator. A certification roadmap is planned as the protocol matures. Organizations with strict compliance requirements are advised to conduct their own technical due diligence and monitor official Cere Network channels for audit status updates.

When a single operator controls k or more nodes within a DDC cluster, that operator possesses sufficient shares to reconstruct the original data independently, without cooperation from other network participants. This threshold represents a meaningful trust boundary in the decentralized architecture. Because DDC distributes trust across smart contracts and network nodes rather than relying on any single certified operator, the protocol's security guarantees depend on no individual operator accumulating control at or beyond this reconstruction threshold. Cluster configurations and operator stake requirements are enforced through smart contracts to reduce the practical likelihood of such concentration occurring.

Under constrained bandwidth conditions typical of edge environments, DDC data repair operations must account for limited connectivity when reconstructing degraded or lost data fragments. Repair processes prioritize critical data segments first, deferring lower-priority fragment reconstruction until sufficient bandwidth is available. Unlike cloud-based Dragon 1 deployments with 48 or more nodes and reliable high-throughput links, edge clusters may operate with reduced repair parallelism, extending the time to full redundancy restoration. Operators should establish clear replication frequency guidelines and acceptable recovery point objectives appropriate to the available network capacity of their specific edge deployment.

For small edge cluster deployments of approximately five nodes, DDC Core maintains latency and integrity guarantees at the path resolution layer even as individual nodes join or leave the cluster. Path traversal and resolution are handled consistently across all participating nodes, ensuring that client-observed response times remain stable during membership changes. Performance baselines should track both average and peak latency, as memory constraints and garbage collection events can introduce measurable variance. Node join and leave simulation testing was validated as of March 29, 2024, confirming that hierarchical content authentication is preserved across dynamic cluster topologies.

When a small edge cluster loses BFT write quorum due to node failures, write availability cannot be restored automatically. An operator must intervene by first confirming which nodes are still reachable and whether the surviving set constitutes a majority. If a majority cannot be formed from the remaining nodes, writes will be blocked until quorum is restored. To restore quorum, the operator must either bring failed nodes back online or, if nodes are permanently lost, reconfigure the cluster membership to reflect a smaller voting set. Note that any reconfiguration must ensure the new write quorum overlaps with prior write quorums to preserve consistency guarantees. Node join and leave operations have been validated to preserve hierarchical content authentication across dynamic cluster topologies, so rejoining recovered nodes follows the standard join procedure.

When a small edge cluster loses BFT write quorum due to node failures, write availability cannot be restored automatically. An operator must intervene by first confirming which nodes are reachable and whether the failure count exceeds the fault tolerance threshold. If the remaining live nodes are insufficient to form quorum, the operator must either recover failed nodes and rejoin them to the cluster, or explicitly reduce the cluster membership to exclude unrecoverable nodes, allowing the surviving nodes to recalculate and satisfy quorum with the reduced set. Write operations remain unavailable until quorum is re-established.

DDC clusters operate without the ability to decrypt stored content on behalf of any requesting party. Encryption and decryption responsibilities remain entirely with the data owner or authorized client applications, not with the cluster nodes themselves. Access control is enforced through external authorization mechanisms, such as on-chain subscription records or NFT-based registries, which signal whether a requesting party is permitted to retrieve content. The cluster validates access conditions against these external systems but does not participate in key management or content decryption at any point in the data retrieval process.

The DDC cluster operates without access to Data Encryption Keys at any point during the data lifecycle. DEKs are never transmitted to or stored by the cluster infrastructure; instead, the cluster evaluates access conditions against external systems while remaining entirely outside the key management and content decryption process. This architectural boundary ensures that even cluster operators cannot access plaintext content, as decryption authority remains exclusively with authorized parties who hold the corresponding DEK through external key management mechanisms.

The data encryption key (DEK) is generated and held exclusively by the client and is never transmitted to or stored by the DDC cluster. Because the cluster retains no copy of the DEK, it cannot decrypt stored content on behalf of any party, including Cere Network operators. No administrative override or cluster-level key recovery mechanism exists. This design ensures that data confidentiality is enforced architecturally rather than by policy, meaning access to plaintext content remains solely under the control of the key-holding client regardless of cluster operator permissions or network governance decisions.

DDC clusters operate without any administrative override or cluster-level key recovery mechanism. The data encryption key (DEK) is held exclusively by the client and is never transmitted to or stored by the cluster. Because the cluster retains no copy of the DEK, it cannot decrypt stored content on behalf of any party, including Cere Network operators. Data confidentiality is therefore enforced architecturally rather than by policy, meaning no operational procedure or privileged access path exists through which cluster participants could recover or expose encrypted content.

Sybil attack resistance in DDC clusters is currently enforced at the policy layer rather than the protocol layer. Cluster operators and network governance cannot access plaintext content due to client-side key control, which provides a baseline confidentiality boundary independent of operator identity. However, protocol-level guarantees of operator diversity — such as cryptographic proof of distinct node identities or stake-weighted Sybil resistance mechanisms — are not yet enforced by the DDC core protocol. Deployments requiring verifiable operator diversity should implement out-of-band vetting procedures until protocol-level Sybil mitigation is formally specified and delivered.

DDC clusters distribute data across multiple nodes as a baseline confidentiality measure; however, this fragmentation-based security model currently relies on deployment-level operator vetting rather than protocol-enforced guarantees. Cryptographic proof of distinct node identities and stake-weighted Sybil resistance mechanisms are not enforced by the DDC core protocol. A single adversarial operator controlling multiple nodes could undermine the confidentiality assumptions implied by fragmentation.

Deployments requiring verifiable operator diversity should implement out-of-band vetting procedures to confirm independent node ownership and operation. Protocol-level Sybil mitigation is under active specification and will be formally documented upon delivery.

Sybil Resistance Roadmap

DDC currently provides a baseline confidentiality boundary through client-side key control, but protocol-level guarantees of operator diversity are not yet enforced by the DDC core protocol. Cryptographic proof of distinct node identities and stake-weighted Sybil resistance mechanisms remain on the development roadmap. Until these controls are implemented, the fragmentation-based security model relies on deployment-level operator vetting rather than protocol-enforced guarantees. Deployments requiring verifiable operator diversity should implement out-of-band identity verification processes. A formal specification and delivery timeline for native Sybil resistance enforcement will be published as the protocol matures.

DDC's data fragmentation across multiple nodes provides meaningful confidentiality boundaries, reinforced by client-side key control that remains independent of operator identity. However, these protections assume operator diversity that the core protocol does not currently enforce. A single operator controlling k or more nodes in a cluster can reconstruct original data independently, bypassing fragmentation entirely. Unlike centralized platforms where trust is explicitly scoped to a single vendor, DDC deployments carry a Sybil attack surface that is not eliminated by decentralized architecture alone. Protocol-level guarantees such as cryptographic proof of distinct node identities or stake-weighted Sybil resistance mechanisms are not yet enforced, and operator independence verification remains a manual, out-of-band process.

Protocol-level Sybil resistance is not currently enforced by the DDC core protocol. Cryptographic proof of distinct node identities and stake-weighted Sybil resistance mechanisms remain unspecified, meaning operator diversity across a cluster cannot be verified at the protocol layer. A single actor may operate multiple nodes that appear independent, undermining the security assumptions of data distribution. Deployments requiring verifiable operator diversity must implement out-of-band vetting procedures until protocol-level Sybil mitigation is formally specified and delivered. A timeline and technical specification for these mechanisms are pending addition to the DDC Core Wiki roadmap.

DDC clusters currently operate without protocol-level enforcement of operator diversity, meaning a single entity controlling multiple storage nodes can satisfy erasure-coded shard distribution requirements while presenting as independent operators. This constitutes a recognized Sybil attack vector. Although client-side encryption ensures that plaintext content remains under the control of the key-holding client regardless of cluster operator permissions or network-level access, data availability and durability guarantees are still contingent on genuine operator independence. Cluster administrators should manually verify operator identity diversity when provisioning nodes. Protocol-level Sybil resistance mechanisms are under evaluation for a future DDC Core release.

DDC clusters operate without protocol-level enforcement of Sybil resistance. Erasure coding schemes are parameterized by k data chunks and m parity chunks, where any k chunks are sufficient to reconstruct the original object. A single operator controlling k or more nodes within a cluster can therefore reconstruct original data independently, without coordinating with any external party or requiring cluster operator permissions. Deployments handling sensitive data should account for this property when selecting cluster operators and configuring reconstruction thresholds, treating operator concentration as a direct factor in confidentiality guarantees.

DDC clusters do not implement a cluster-level key recovery mechanism. Encryption keys are managed exclusively by the client, meaning access to plaintext content remains solely under the control of the key-holding party regardless of cluster operator permissions or network governance decisions. If a client loses their encryption key, neither cluster operators nor network governance processes can recover access to the encrypted content. Applications integrating with DDC should implement their own key management and backup strategies appropriate to their operational requirements.

DDC clusters retain no copy of the data encryption key (DEK) at any point. The DEK is held exclusively by the client and is never transmitted to or stored by the cluster, meaning the cluster cannot decrypt stored content on behalf of any party, including Cere Network operators. No administrative override or cluster-level key recovery mechanism exists. Clients are solely responsible for safeguarding their DEKs. Loss of a DEK results in permanent inaccessibility of the associated data, with no recovery path available through the cluster or network infrastructure.

DDC clusters operate without access to the decryption keys required to read stored content. Encryption and key management are handled exclusively at the application layer, meaning neither cluster nodes nor Cere Network operators can access plaintext data on behalf of any party. This design ensures that data sovereignty remains with the user or integrating application at all times, consistent with DDC's broader principle of eliminating reliance on any single privileged service provider. Integrators should implement and retain their own key management strategies independently of cluster infrastructure.

The data encryption key (DEK) is generated and held exclusively by the client and is never transmitted to or stored by the DDC cluster. Because the cluster retains no copy of the DEK, it cannot decrypt stored content on behalf of any party, including Cere Network operators. No administrative override or cluster-level key recovery mechanism exists. This design ensures that data confidentiality is enforced architecturally rather than by policy, meaning access to plaintext content remains solely under the control of the key-holding client regardless of cluster operator permissions or network-level access.

Client-side encryption in DDC depends entirely on correct implementation within the application layer. If an application has a bug or fails to encrypt certain records before writing to DDC, those payloads are stored as plaintext on cluster nodes with no automatic rejection or validation at the protocol level. Operators and auditors should periodically inspect node-stored payloads directly to verify that content is not readable as plaintext. Where client-side encryption cannot be guaranteed, applications should fall back to server-side encryption using the same DEK derivation process to ensure no unencrypted data reaches the cluster.

DDC nodes are designed to continue serving storage and compute requests even when the blockchain is temporarily unavailable. Because authorization state and bucket permissions are enforced at the node level using signed requests verified against user identity, nodes can authenticate and process operations independently of live chain connectivity. The blockchain functions as an immutable record for validation results and cluster coordination, but this role is decoupled from the data path. This separation ensures that transient blockchain outages do not interrupt active data operations within the cluster.

DDC edge clusters require a strict majority quorum to remain operational. For a 5-node cluster, at least 3 nodes must be available to satisfy both read and write quorum. A 7-node cluster requires 4 nodes, and a 10-node cluster requires 6 nodes. Write operations enforce quorum at all times to preserve consistency; read operations apply the same threshold to guarantee that returned data reflects the latest acknowledged write. Clusters that fall below these thresholds suspend operations to prevent split-brain conditions, consistent with Byzantine fault tolerance principles underlying DDC quorum design.

DDC clusters distribute data across peer-to-peer nodes, eliminating the single points of failure inherent in centralized storage architectures. Edge and hybrid cluster configurations target 99.9% or higher availability by ensuring no individual node represents a critical dependency; workloads are automatically redistributed when a node becomes unreachable. This contrasts with centralized alternatives, where a provider outage or infrastructure failure can render data inaccessible until recovery is complete. By design, DDC's decentralized topology reduces mean time to recovery at the cluster level, as redundant peers continue serving requests without requiring operator intervention or scheduled maintenance windows.

When a network partition separates edge-local node groups from broader regional participants in a hybrid cluster, DDC applies availability-favoring behavior consistent with CAP theorem trade-offs: edge nodes continue serving local client operations independently rather than blocking on cross-partition consensus. Once connectivity is restored, the cluster reconciles divergent state through its replication protocol. To reduce split-brain exposure, hybrid cluster deployments should provision redundant communication links between edge and regional node groups, ensuring that transient failures do not trigger unnecessary partition handling and that full cluster coordination resumes with minimal data inconsistency upon reconnection.

Each node in a DDC cluster retains only a portion of the complete dataset rather than a full replica. DDC Core functions as an isolated software instance that enables a node to join a cluster and collaborate with peer nodes, meaning the operator of any individual node has direct visibility into only the data fragments stored locally on that node. This partial storage model is an inherent property of the distributed architecture, and full dataset reconstruction requires coordination across the cluster rather than access to any single node.

DDC clusters maintain continuous request routing even when individual nodes become unavailable. As storage nodes go offline, the cluster automatically redirects client requests to remaining active nodes, ensuring uninterrupted data access. This behavior is supported by erasure coding, which splits data into encoded chunks distributed across multiple storage nodes. Because the underlying content is recoverable from a subset of those chunks, the failure of any single node does not prevent the cluster from fulfilling read or write operations, and clients experience no interruption during node-level failures.

DDC clusters distribute data by fragmenting files and assigning portions across multiple storage nodes, ensuring no single node holds a complete copy of any given dataset. Because retrieval depends on coordinated access across participating nodes rather than any individual endpoint, the failure or degradation of one node does not create a bottleneck for latency or availability. This architecture means no single node represents a critical path, allowing the cluster to continue serving requests as long as a sufficient set of participating nodes remains reachable within the network.

Storage and cache nodes maintain bucket and customer information locally, enabling continued operation during periods of blockchain unavailability. This local state persistence allows nodes to serve read and write requests without interruption even when connectivity to the Cere Blockchain is temporarily lost. The blockchain functions as an immutable repository for validation results and authoritative cluster state, but node-level operations do not require a live blockchain connection to function correctly. Operators should account for this behavior when designing availability and recovery procedures for DDC deployments.

DDC's data fragmentation across multiple nodes provides meaningful confidentiality boundaries, reinforced by client-side key control that remains independent of operator identity. However, these protections assume operator diversity that the core protocol does not currently enforce. A single operator controlling k or more nodes in a cluster can reconstruct original data independently, bypassing fragmentation entirely. Unlike centralized platforms where trust is explicitly scoped to a single vendor, DDC deployments carry a Sybil attack surface that is not eliminated by decentralized architecture alone. Protocol-level guarantees such as cryptographic proof of distinct node identities or stake-weighted Sybil resistance mechanisms are not yet enforced, and operator independence verification remains a manual, out-of-band process.

When a single operator controls k or more nodes within a DDC cluster, that operator possesses sufficient shares to reconstruct the original data independently, without cooperation from other network participants. This threshold represents a meaningful trust boundary in the decentralized architecture. Because DDC distributes trust across smart contracts and network nodes rather than relying on any single certified operator, the protocol's security guarantees depend on no individual operator accumulating control at or beyond this reconstruction threshold. Cluster configurations and operator stake requirements are enforced through smart contracts to reduce the practical likelihood of such concentration occurring.

DDC storage and cache nodes are designed to continue serving requests even when the blockchain is temporarily unavailable. Authorization state and bucket permissions are enforced at the node level, allowing storage and compute operations to proceed without requiring a live blockchain connection for every request. The blockchain functions as an immutable repository for validation results and cluster configuration rather than as a real-time gatekeeper, enabling the distributed storage layer to maintain availability and Byzantine fault tolerance independently of blockchain connectivity during transient outages.

DDC clusters maintain read and write availability during single-node failures by distributing content across multiple storage nodes as erasure-coded chunks, allowing the cluster to reconstruct any object from a sufficient subset of those chunks. Clients experience no interruption to ongoing operations when an individual node becomes unavailable.
