An autonomous edge cluster in DDC is a cluster capable of independently executing storage, routing, compute, and request-level authentication functions without requiring live connectivity to the blockchain or any external coordination service. This capability is achieved through a combination of disk-persisted indexes, Kademlia DHT-based routing, and local authentication enforcement. Critically, autonomy does not imply privileged data access: the cluster operates on encrypted user data and enforces permissions defined by the data owner through token or NFT ownership records, ensuring no infrastructure operator can unilaterally access content outside those explicitly granted permissions.

When a network partition heals and edge-local nodes rejoin the broader hybrid cluster, DDC initiates state reconciliation using CRDT (Conflict-free Replicated Data Type) semantics. Under this model, concurrent writes made during the partition period are merged deterministically without requiring coordinator arbitration, ensuring zero data loss by construction. For disjoint write regions where collision probability approaches zero, automatic merge succeeds with probability exceeding 0.99. Where write regions overlap and conflicts cannot be resolved automatically, the replication protocol falls back to last-write-wins ordering based on node-attached logical timestamps, with the resolved state propagated to all cluster participants before resuming normal operation.

DDC clusters distribute data as encoded chunks across multiple storage nodes, ensuring that the full content remains recoverable from any sufficient subset of those chunks. Because no single node holds an irreplaceable portion of the data, the failure of an individual node does not prevent the cluster from fulfilling read or write operations. Clients experience no interruption during node-level failures, as the cluster continues to serve requests by drawing on the remaining available nodes without requiring manual intervention or client-side retry logic.

DDC clusters operate without access to the decryption keys required to read stored content. Encryption and key management are handled exclusively at the application layer, meaning neither cluster nodes nor Cere Network operators can access plaintext data on behalf of any party. This design ensures that data sovereignty remains with the user or integrating application at all times, consistent with DDC's broader principle of eliminating reliance on any single privileged service provider. Integrators should implement and retain their own key management strategies independently of cluster infrastructure.

DDC provides access control enforcement at the protocol level, where only cryptographically authorized requests are processed by CDN and storage nodes, and granular permissions can be scoped to individual buckets, pieces, or collections. These properties are enforced by smart contracts rather than a centralized policy engine. Centralized platforms such as Snowflake and Databricks offer complementary advantages including default encryption at rest and in transit, TLS enforcement, and established compliance certifications such as SOC 2 and ISO 27001. Organizations evaluating DDC should weigh decentralized authorization guarantees against the maturity of compliance tooling currently available in traditional data warehouse offerings.

Sybil Resistance Roadmap

DDC currently provides a baseline confidentiality boundary through client-side key control, but protocol-level guarantees of operator diversity are not yet enforced by the DDC core protocol. Cryptographic proof of distinct node identities and stake-weighted Sybil resistance mechanisms remain on the development roadmap. Until these controls are implemented, the fragmentation-based security model relies on deployment-level operator vetting rather than protocol-enforced guarantees. Deployments requiring verifiable operator diversity should implement out-of-band identity verification processes. A formal specification and delivery timeline for native Sybil resistance enforcement will be published as the protocol matures.

DDC's security model addresses three concrete threat vectors that centralized storage cannot mitigate by design. In an insider access scenario, infrastructure operators have no privileged decryption capability, meaning a rogue administrator cannot unilaterally retrieve user data. In a provider subpoena scenario, compelled disclosure is constrained to what the cluster itself can access, which excludes plaintext content where ownership and access rights are enforced externally via smart contract or NFT registry. In a data breach scenario, the absence of a central privileged access path eliminates the single-point extraction risk that makes centralized breaches catastrophic at scale.

The data encryption key (DEK) is generated and held exclusively by the client and is never transmitted to or stored by any node within the DDC cluster. Clients are responsible for retaining access to the corresponding DEK through external key management mechanisms of their choosing. Because no cluster node holds a copy of the DEK, the cluster cannot decrypt stored content on behalf of any party. There is no administrative override or cluster-level key recovery path available to DDC operators or Cere Network.

DDC clusters operate without any administrative override or cluster-level key recovery mechanism. The data encryption key (DEK) is held exclusively by the client and is never transmitted to or stored by the cluster. Because the cluster retains no copy of the DEK, it cannot decrypt stored content on behalf of any party, including Cere Network operators. Data confidentiality is therefore enforced architecturally rather than by policy, meaning no operational procedure or privileged access path exists through which cluster participants could recover or expose encrypted content.

In edge deployments operating under constrained or unreliable bandwidth, data repair timing and parallelism characteristics differ significantly from those observed in full-size clusters. Benchmark data for a representative 5-node edge cluster is pending publication in the DDC Core Wiki; until that data is available, operators should not assume repair throughput comparable to full infrastructure benchmarks. SLA targets for edge repair operations should be defined per deployment based on available node count, measured bandwidth, and acceptable data unavailability windows, and reviewed whenever cluster topology or connectivity conditions change.

Note: The internal evidence provided does not contain sufficient technical detail about DDC's quorum mechanics, write transaction handling, or rollback behavior to support factually grounded documentation. Writing specific technical claims about atomicity guarantees, rollback procedures, or partial-write behavior without that evidence would risk introducing inaccurate information into your documentation.

To write this section accurately, please provide internal sources describing DDC's consensus or quorum protocol, write path architecture, or any existing error handling specifications for edge cluster operations.

DDC clusters operate without a single point of failure by design. When data is ingested, the system fragments it and distributes portions across individual storage nodes within the cluster, ensuring no single node holds a complete copy of any given file. Data retrieval is therefore coordinated across multiple participating nodes rather than routed through any one critical path. This distribution model maintains availability across the cluster while reducing redundancy overhead, so the failure or removal of an individual node does not interrupt access to stored data.

Edge cluster repair performance benchmarks for 5-node configurations are currently pending publication. Until this data is available, operators should treat SLA targets for fault recovery scenarios as provisional and avoid committing to contractual recovery time objectives based on unverified estimates.

Operators sizing edge cluster deployments can reference the connection limits documented for edge cluster onboarding as a baseline for capacity planning: a maximum of 5000 active HTTP requests, 2048 queued pending requests, and a defined ceiling on simultaneous TCP connections. These parameters constrain throughput during degraded states and should inform conservative SLA modeling until formal repair benchmarks are released.

When a storage or compute node becomes unavailable, the DDC Orchestrator detects the failure and initiates workload redistribution across the remaining healthy nodes in the cluster. This process is governed by assignment logic configured within the Orchestrator, which continuously monitors node state and reallocates tasks without requiring manual intervention.

Redistribution is triggered by node health signals surfaced through the cluster execution layer. The Orchestrator then applies distribution rules to reassign affected workloads to available nodes, maintaining continuity of service. This behavior is a property of the cluster architecture rather than any single application layer.

Security Comparison Disclaimer

The security characteristics described in this section reflect DDC's architecture as documented internally and should not be interpreted as independently verified assessments of third-party platforms. Specific claims regarding the security controls of named vendors such as Databricks or Snowflake have not been validated against current product documentation and may not accurately reflect those platforms' current capabilities. Readers should consult vendor documentation directly before drawing comparative conclusions.

Regarding encryption, DDC does not enforce encryption at rest by default. Data stored within a cluster remains in plaintext unless the client application explicitly encrypts it prior to upload. Any documentation stating otherwise should be treated as inaccurate until corrected through a formal review cycle.

DDC clusters distribute data across multiple nodes as a baseline confidentiality measure; however, this fragmentation-based security model currently relies on deployment-level operator vetting rather than protocol-enforced guarantees. Cryptographic proof of distinct node identities and stake-weighted Sybil resistance mechanisms are not enforced by the DDC core protocol. A single adversarial operator controlling multiple nodes could undermine the confidentiality assumptions implied by fragmentation.

Deployments requiring verifiable operator diversity should implement out-of-band vetting procedures to confirm independent node ownership and operation. Protocol-level Sybil mitigation is under active specification and will be formally documented upon delivery.

Transport Layer Security (TLS) is not enforced by default across DDC storage and CDN nodes. As a result, data-in-transit confidentiality is not guaranteed unless TLS is explicitly configured for all node communications. Operators deploying DDC in security-sensitive environments should ensure TLS 1.2 or higher is enabled, as earlier versions lack modern protections and are considered insufficient for production use. Note that while DDC enforces cryptographic access control at the protocol level, this does not substitute for transport encryption and should not be interpreted as providing equivalent data-in-transit protection.

During node failure events, client read latency in DDC is influenced by whether requested content is available at the CDN layer or must be retrieved from the storage layer. When a CDN node cannot serve cached content, requests are proxied to the storage layer, introducing additional round-trip overhead. Latency outcomes depend on node lifetime estimates and cluster state propagation delays at the time of failure. Quantitative measurements from Milestone 1 simulations are pending publication; the table below will be populated with empirical read latency data once those results are formally recorded.

In the 16/48 erasure coding scheme, a DDC cluster tolerates the simultaneous loss of up to 32 storage nodes without data loss. This threshold is derived directly from the distribution parameters: because any 16 of the 48 chunks are sufficient to reconstruct the original data, the remaining 32 chunks may be unavailable without affecting recoverability. Operators should treat 32 as the maximum simultaneous node failure budget under this scheme. Deployments requiring a higher fault tolerance margin must be configured with a proportionally larger chunk distribution ratio.

DDC edge clusters are designed to continue serving read and write operations during periods of blockchain unavailability by relying on disk-persisted indexes and locally cached authentication state. However, this autonomy is bounded in practice: permission data derived from on-chain state becomes increasingly stale over time, meaning that revoked keys or updated access policies will not be reflected until blockchain connectivity is restored. Clusters operating in a disconnected state should be considered degraded rather than fully equivalent to connected operation, and reconnection is required to re-validate account balances, DDC deposits, and any permission changes made during the offline interval.

Your data wallet is the sr25519 keypair generated during the initialization step. It serves as your cryptographic identity for all DDC operations, including authenticating requests, signing transactions, and controlling access to stored data. The seed phrase produced by this command is the human-readable representation of that keypair; treat it as a private credential, because anyone who possesses it can assume your identity within the cluster.

Retain the seed phrase in a secure location before proceeding. It cannot be recovered from the network if lost, and all data access permissions are bound exclusively to the keypair it represents.

Your data wallet is the sr25519 keypair generated when you create a DDC account. It serves as your cryptographic identity for all DDC operations, including storing, retrieving, and managing data across the cluster. Every data interaction within DDC is attributed to this keypair, ensuring that access control, billing, and audit trails are consistently tied to a single verifiable identity. When you sign a DDC transaction or authorize a data request, your data wallet keypair produces the signature that nodes use to authenticate and attribute the operation to your account.

When multiple DDC nodes become unavailable, client request latency may remain unchanged because requests are routed through try points that provide access to hierarchical content paths rather than direct node addresses. A client requesting content by path does not resolve individual node availability at the CID level, so the routing layer can continue serving requests through remaining accessible nodes without exposing latency changes to the client. This behavior is a consequence of path-based content addressing, where the client holds access to a path segment without requiring knowledge of the underlying content identifier.

DDC nodes operate as distributed access points within a cluster, where each node holds references to content addressable data organized in hierarchical paths. When a client requests content by path, the cluster routes the request across available nodes using the path structure rather than direct CID resolution. Because multiple nodes maintain access to the same path segments, the removal of individual nodes does not interrupt request routing or measurably affect client-perceived latency, as the cluster continues to serve requests through remaining nodes without renegotiating access to the underlying content hierarchy.

DDC nodes operate with built-in redundancy across the cluster, meaning that when individual nodes are taken offline, request routing continues through remaining active nodes without interruption to the client. Because authorization details are attached at the request level and validated independently by whichever node handles the request, no single node represents a critical path for latency or availability. Clients interacting with the cluster therefore observe consistent response times regardless of partial node failures, as the decentralized architecture distributes load and maintains service continuity across the remaining cluster members.

Because authorization details are attached at the request level and validated independently by whichever node handles the request, no single node represents a critical path for latency or availability. Clients interacting with the cluster therefore observe consistent response times regardless of partial node failures, as the decentralized architecture distributes load and maintains service continuity across the remaining healthy nodes. This design ensures that localized failures do not propagate into client-visible degradation, allowing applications built on DDC to meet availability requirements without implementing additional failover logic at the application layer.

DDC edge cluster data repair performance is dependent on cluster size, available bandwidth, and the number of pieces requiring reconstruction. As a reference point, full infrastructure deployments have completed repair of approximately 600,000 pieces in around 20 hours under representative conditions. For 5-node edge cluster deployments operating under constrained bandwidth, repair times will vary significantly from this baseline. Enterprise operators defining recovery point objectives should account for these variables when establishing SLA targets, and are advised to conduct repair benchmarking under their specific network conditions until standardized edge cluster SLA targets are published.

Edge cluster deployments operate under topology-specific availability constraints that differ from hybrid configurations. The published 99.9% availability target applies to edge and hybrid configurations collectively and should not be used as a per-topology SLA baseline for planning purposes. Enterprises deploying minimum-size edge clusters should conduct benchmarking under their specific network conditions until standardized edge cluster SLA targets are published. Assuming 99.9% availability independently for a small edge cluster may result in under-provisioned redundancy and unmet uptime expectations in production environments.

This mechanism ensures that data uploaded before a given node existed remains accessible through the network without requiring a full data replica on every node.

DDC Core Wiki 33

Storage and cache nodes maintain a nodes index that is persisted to disk, enabling continued operation even when the blockchain is temporarily unavailable. This means that if nodes are restarted under such conditions, they can resume authenticating clients and serving requests without errors, drawing on the locally persisted index rather than requiring a live blockchain connection. The routing table will retry blockchain connectivity in the background, but node functionality is not blocked pending its restoration. This design ensures resilience against transient blockchain outages without compromising client authentication or data access.

DDC storage and cache nodes maintain a nodes index that is persisted to disk, enabling continued operation even when the blockchain is temporarily unavailable. This means that following a node restart, authentication and routing can proceed using the locally stored index without requiring an active blockchain connection. The routing table employs a retry mechanism during blockchain unavailability, and once the persisted index is loaded, nodes can resume serving authenticated clients without errors, ensuring resilience against transient blockchain outages.

Storage and cache nodes maintain a local, disk-persisted index of the nodes registry, synchronized from the blockchain during normal operation. If the blockchain becomes unavailable, the routing table enters a retry loop and cannot initialize until connectivity is restored. However, nodes that have already written this index to disk can be restarted and continue functioning without errors, as they fall back to the locally persisted state. This design ensures that temporary blockchain unavailability does not result in complete service disruption for nodes that have previously completed synchronization.

Storage and cache nodes maintain a nodes index that is persisted on disk, enabling continued operation even when the blockchain is temporarily unavailable. This means that if nodes are restarted under such conditions, they can resume authenticating clients and functioning without errors, drawing on the locally stored index rather than requiring a live blockchain connection. In cases where the blockchain is unreachable, the routing table will retry until connectivity is restored, but storage and cache node authentication remains uninterrupted through the persisted index.

Storage and cache nodes maintain a nodes index that is persisted to disk, enabling continued operation even when the blockchain is temporarily unavailable. If a node restarts under these conditions, it loads the persisted index and resumes authentication and routing functions without errors, rather than entering a retry loop waiting for blockchain connectivity to be restored.

This design ensures that client authentication remains functional during blockchain outages, as the node can reference its locally stored index to validate requests. The persisted index serves as a resilient fallback, preserving service continuity across restarts without requiring an active blockchain connection.

Storage and cache nodes maintain a local, disk-persisted index of cluster nodes, allowing them to restart and resume normal operation even when the blockchain is temporarily unavailable. This persistence ensures continuity of storage functions without dependency on live blockchain connectivity at startup.

When the blockchain is unreachable, the routing table enters a retry loop and cannot initialize until connectivity is restored. To mitigate this, the nodes index is written to disk so that previously synchronized topology data remains accessible, decoupling node startup from real-time blockchain availability and preserving cluster operability during transient network or chain outages.

Network supports 3rd party nodes onboarding. There are easy to understand
guides on how to launch and support node. 3rd party nodes canʼt break or hurt
the network. At this decentralisation phase Cere controls data distribution
mechanism and holds enough data to serve client needs even in case there are

gaps. It will also include protection mechanism, tests, stability improvement.

DDC Core Wiki 66

DDC supports two additional deployment topologies beyond standard regional clusters: edge clusters and hybrid clusters. An edge cluster is a minimal-footprint deployment optimized for low-latency data access at the network boundary, requiring a minimum of 5 nodes while maintaining core fault tolerance guarantees. A hybrid cluster combines edge and regional node configurations within a single logical cluster, enabling workloads to benefit from both local processing proximity and broader regional redundancy. These topologies share the same underlying DDC consensus and replication mechanisms but differ in their geographic distribution assumptions and failure domain boundaries.

Operator Diversity and Sybil Attack Mitigation

DDC clusters depend on a distributed set of independent node operators to uphold data availability and integrity guarantees. If a single operator gains control of a threshold number of nodes within a cluster, the assumptions underlying replication and consensus are undermined, potentially exposing stored data to coordinated withholding, censorship, or unauthorized access. The protocol currently identifies this as a known risk. Cluster administrators and application developers are advised to verify operator diversity manually when selecting or forming clusters, as automated on-chain monitoring tooling for detecting operator concentration is not yet available.

Operator diversity audits for edge clusters should be conducted on a quarterly basis, with automated triggers generated from blockchain indexer events such as node registration changes, stake reallocation, or cluster membership updates. When the indexer emits these events, operators should cross-reference the current node provider distribution against the cluster's diversity thresholds before the next epoch boundary. Audit results should be logged against the corresponding on-chain block height to maintain a verifiable record of compliance over time, enabling enterprise operators to demonstrate sustained independence rather than point-in-time conformance.

DDC clusters operate without a centralized coordinator, relying on distributed protocols for all routing, data placement, and replication decisions. To manage network topology, DDC adopts the industry-standard Kademlia DHT protocol, replacing an earlier custom Hash Ring-based solution. Kademlia DHT enables each node to independently resolve routing paths and maintain balanced data distribution across the cluster, ensuring that data plane operations remain available even when control plane or blockchain connectivity is interrupted. This architecture eliminates single points of failure and allows the cluster to scale without requiring centralized orchestration.

DDC clusters operate without a centralized coordinator. All routing, data placement, and access control decisions are made by individual nodes using distributed protocols, including the industry-standard Kademlia DHT for topology management. This architecture ensures that data plane operations remain unaffected by control plane or blockchain unavailability; when blockchain state is temporarily inaccessible, nodes authenticate requests using locally cached cluster configuration, preserving continuous operation. The result is a system with no single point of failure or vendor dependency, granting application owners direct control over their data without reliance on any privileged coordinating service.

Platform Comparison Disclaimer

The capability comparisons referenced in this documentation reflect DDC's own design specifications and architectural properties only. Claims regarding Databricks, Snowflake, or other named platforms are provided for general orientation and have not been validated against those vendors' current documentation or contractual guarantees. Readers evaluating DDC against alternative platforms should consult the respective vendor documentation directly to verify security, availability, and compliance claims. Cere Network makes no authoritative representation regarding the capabilities of third-party platforms.

Erasure coding in DDC provides fault tolerance and availability by splitting data into fragments distributed across cluster nodes, but it does not provide confidentiality. Individual node operators can observe the content of the fragments they store. The scheme is parameterized to balance storage overhead against redundancy, not to obscure data. Confidentiality at the storage layer requires explicit encryption applied to data before erasure coding and distribution. Claims that erasure-coded fragments are unreadable to cluster operators are incorrect and should not be relied upon as a security property.

Data stored in DDC is plaintext by default. The comparison table entry "Encryption at rest: Default, enforced at node level" is incorrect and should be read as "Client-side only; plaintext by default." Erasure coding distributes data across nodes as an availability and integrity mechanism, not a confidentiality one; cluster operators can read fragment contents, and this should not be relied upon as a security property.

SDK-integrated encryption is planned and will enable client-side encryption before data reaches the cluster. Until that capability is available, applications requiring confidentiality must implement their own encryption prior to upload.

DDC does not enforce encryption at rest at the node level. Data is stored in plaintext by default across DDC storage nodes. The comparison table entry "Default, enforced at node level" is incorrect and should be read as "Client-side only; plaintext by default." Encryption at rest is the responsibility of the application or client layer. Developers requiring confidentiality of stored data must apply encryption before upload using the client-side key management primitives described in the Encrypted Data Access and Key Delegation specification. Node operators have no obligation to enforce or verify encryption of stored content.

DDC nodes never process or receive user seed phrases. Authentication is handled client-side: the SDK derives a signing key from the user's seed or wallet locally, then uses that key to sign storage and compute requests before they are transmitted to DDC nodes. Nodes verify the cryptographic signature against the declared identity without ever having access to the underlying seed material. Authorization is subsequently enforced at the node level based on bucket permissions and on-chain policy, keeping credential material strictly within the client boundary throughout the request lifecycle.

DDC nodes never process or receive user seed phrases. Authentication is handled client-side: the SDK derives a signing key from the user's seed or wallet locally, then uses that key to sign storage and compute requests before they are transmitted to DDC nodes. Nodes verify the cryptographic signature against the declared identity without ever seeing the underlying credential. Authorization is subsequently enforced by the node based on bucket permissions and on-chain policy, keeping the seed phrase strictly within the client environment throughout the entire request lifecycle.

DDC nodes store data in plaintext by default. Access control is enforced at the bucket level through on-chain permissions, where buckets are designated as either public or private. Storage requests are authenticated by requiring the client to sign each request with a user key derived from a seed or wallet, and nodes verify this signature before granting access. Sensitive data requiring confidentiality should therefore be encrypted client-side before upload, as the storage layer itself does not apply encryption to content at rest.

DDC nodes join the network and authenticate through a decentralized identity model rather than centralized coordination. Each node verifies incoming storage and compute requests by validating cryptographic signatures tied to user keys derived from a seed or wallet. Authorization decisions are enforced locally on the node based on bucket permissions defined on-chain, allowing nodes to independently confirm access rights without consulting a central authority. This design enables nodes to participate in the cluster and serve requests in a trustless manner, consistent with the broader DDC architecture of combining smart contracts with decentralized storage infrastructure.

DDC nodes process and fulfill storage requests autonomously by verifying the identity and authorization of each incoming request without requiring centralized coordination. When a client submits a storage or compute request, the node independently validates the request signature against the submitting identity and enforces access control based on bucket permissions defined on-chain. This allows nodes to accept or reject requests and serve data without delegating those decisions to an external authority, enabling the cluster to operate in a trustless and self-sufficient manner.

DDC nodes process storage and compute requests autonomously by verifying requests against on-chain authorization rules without requiring centralized coordination. When a client submits a request, the node validates the cryptographic signature tied to the user's identity and checks bucket-level permissions defined on-chain before fulfilling the operation. This enforcement model allows individual nodes to make access and execution decisions independently, ensuring that data operations remain trustless and consistent with the permissions established at the smart contract layer.

DDC nodes validate authorization at the point of request rather than maintaining continuous chain synchronization. When a storage or compute request arrives, the node verifies the request signature against the submitting identity and consults on-chain bucket permissions only as needed to confirm whether access is public or private. This approach keeps nodes operationally independent from the chain during normal data handling while ensuring that permission enforcement remains grounded in authoritative on-chain state at the moment of each access decision.

When a client submits a request to a CDN or storage node, the client attaches authorization details, typically in the form of a request signature, directly to the request payload. The node uses these authorization details to verify the client's identity and authorize the request before processing it. Any request that fails this verification is rejected by the node. Authorization details may also be retained by the node to support activity capture, enabling auditors or validators to confirm that the client was legitimately authorized at the time the request was made.

DDC clusters operate without the ability to decrypt stored content on behalf of any requesting party. Encryption and access control are governed entirely by the data owner's wallet keypair, which is used to sign operations, authorize access to encrypted data, and delegate permissions to other parties. Because decryption authority is never held or exercised by the cluster itself, no node or cluster operator can retrieve plaintext content on a user's behalf. This design ensures that trust in data interactions across DDC clusters is anchored exclusively to the owner's data wallet, not to infrastructure-level actors.

DDC nodes authenticate incoming storage and compute requests by verifying cryptographic signatures, not by receiving or processing user credentials. The SDK derives a signing key from the user's seed phrase or wallet entirely on the client side, and only the resulting signed request is transmitted to DDC nodes. Nodes validate that signature against the declared identity and enforce authorization based on bucket permissions and on-chain policy. Seed phrases and raw wallet secrets remain local to the client at all times and are never exposed to the DDC infrastructure.

DDC nodes store data in plaintext by default. Encryption is not applied at the infrastructure layer; instead, data protection is the responsibility of the client. Bucket permissions, enforced on-chain, control access as either public or private, and storage requests are authenticated by node-verified signatures derived from the user's key. Applications requiring confidentiality should encrypt data client-side before submission to DDC nodes, ensuring that plaintext content is never transmitted or stored without prior encryption at the application layer.

In edge-constrained environments, DDC data repair windows extend significantly beyond the approximately 20 hours observed on full-size infrastructure for a node holding 600,000 pieces. Available uplink bandwidth is the primary bottleneck; operators should expect repair duration to scale inversely with sustained throughput. To reduce recovery time, configure repair parallelism conservatively to avoid saturating limited links while still making forward progress. Where bandwidth is severely restricted, prioritize repair of high-access-frequency pieces first by adjusting piece selection policy, and schedule repair operations during off-peak periods to minimize contention with active read and write traffic.

Critical Warning: Key Loss Results in Permanent Data Loss

DDC encrypts data client-side before submission to cluster nodes, meaning plaintext content is never stored or transmitted without prior encryption at the application layer. The Data Encryption Key (DEK) and any associated seed phrase are the sole credentials required to decrypt stored content. No administrative override or cluster-level key recovery mechanism exists within DDC. If a DEK or seed phrase is lost, all data encrypted under that key becomes permanently and irrecoverably inaccessible. Users and application developers must implement independent, secure key backup procedures before storing data in DDC.

DDC enforces a client-side encryption requirement as a baseline security control: applications must encrypt data before submission to DDC nodes, ensuring plaintext content is never transmitted or stored without prior encryption at the application layer. This control maps to the NIST Cybersecurity Framework's Protect function, specifically the Data Security category (PR.DS). Security evaluations of DDC deployments should treat client-side encryption as a verifiable precondition, testable by inspecting node-stored payloads for plaintext content. Fragmentation-based distribution provides an additional confidentiality layer but is not a substitute for this mandatory encryption requirement.

In edge deployments operating under reduced bandwidth and limited node counts, data repair behavior differs from full-cluster environments. Repair parallelism is constrained by available inter-node throughput, meaning that simultaneous repair operations must be throttled to avoid saturating the network path. Repair scheduling in these environments should be treated as a background process with lower concurrency limits than standard cluster configurations. Operators deploying edge clusters should expect longer repair completion windows and configure monitoring thresholds accordingly, rather than applying the same timing expectations used for full-size DDC cluster deployments.

When an application's DDC deposit balance is exhausted, the associated bucket enters a suspended state in which write access is immediately revoked while existing data remains temporarily accessible for retrieval. If the deposit is not replenished within the protocol-defined grace period, the bucket transitions to a terminated state and stored data becomes eligible for garbage collection by cluster nodes. Applications dependent on continuous data availability should maintain sufficient deposit reserves and monitor balance thresholds proactively, as data recovery after termination is not guaranteed by the protocol.

Authentication in DDC is handled entirely on the client side. The SDK derives a signing key from the user's seed phrase or wallet locally, and all blockchain transactions and DDC storage or compute requests are signed using this identity before being transmitted. DDC nodes receive and verify these signed requests but are never exposed to the underlying seed phrase or wallet credentials. Authorization is then enforced at the node level based on bucket permissions and on-chain policy, ensuring that sensitive key material remains exclusively within the client environment throughout the request lifecycle.

An edge cluster is a DDC node grouping deployed at the network boundary, closer to data sources or end users, to reduce latency and support regional data locality. A hybrid cluster combines edge-positioned nodes with nodes in a central or cloud-hosted region, enabling data redundancy across deployment tiers. Both topologies operate within the standard DDC cluster model and remain subject to the same erasure coding and replication constraints as any other cluster configuration. Minimum node counts and erasure coding parameters should follow the baseline requirements defined for standard DDC clusters unless a specific edge deployment profile is documented separately.

By default, DDC cluster configurations transmit and store data in plaintext. Operators deploying standard clusters should be aware that this default behavior affects any tooling in the data path, including version-control integrations, build pipelines, and automation gateways, all of which will process content as unencoded character data. Erasure coding and replication constraints apply equally to plaintext configurations; minimum node counts and erasure coding parameters must still satisfy the baseline requirements defined for standard DDC clusters unless a dedicated edge deployment profile has been explicitly documented and applied.

DDC nodes can authenticate clients even when customer information is not present in the local cache by leveraging a layer of trust established through signed requests. When a client generates a pre-signed URL, the DDC protocol captures activity through signed request and acknowledgment pairs, enabling nodes to validate the client's identity and record network participation for reward purposes. In deployments where nodes operate under a designated DNS name, clients may embed both an access token and a signed payload within the shared URL, allowing nodes to verify authenticity without requiring a prior cache lookup of customer data.

Before trusting a DDC cluster with sensitive data, operators should be manually verified for independence by inspecting the cluster's node registry on-chain and confirming that node operators correspond to distinct controlling accounts with no shared issuers in their authentication token chains. Because DDC's trust chain model derives authority from token issuers at the pallet level, a cluster where multiple nodes share a common issuer or subject lineage may indicate overlapping control, reducing the effective isolation that data fragmentation is intended to provide. Protocol-level Sybil resistance mechanisms are under active development and are not yet enforced automatically.

DDC's threat model addresses compromised storage nodes through client-side encryption, ensuring nodes never process plaintext data and keys are never transmitted to the storage layer. Data fragmentation across nodes provides additional isolation, though subject lineage relationships may indicate overlapping control between nodes, which can reduce the effective isolation this architecture is intended to provide. Protocol-level Sybil resistance mechanisms are under active development and are not yet automatically enforced, representing a residual risk that enterprise teams should account for when evaluating DDC deployments against their own security requirements.

Additional threat mitigations covering Sybil resistance, TLS layer guarantees, and on-chain access grant management are under active development, with storage node blockchain query integration and migration tooling listed as pending implementation. A formal published threat model document consolidating current mitigations, known residual risks, and remediation timelines is not yet available. Enterprise teams requiring independent security assessment should contact Cere Network directly for current security posture documentation pending public disclosure.

Authorization is then enforced according to bucket-level permissions, which are defined on-chain as either public or private, ensuring that access control decisions are grounded in verifiable blockchain state rather than client-supplied claims.

DDC's security posture differs from centralized platforms such as Snowflake in ways that depend significantly on client implementation choices rather than platform enforcement. Encryption at rest and in transit is not applied by default; applications using the DDC SDK are responsible for encrypting data prior to upload and for establishing secure transport configurations. Centralized warehousing platforms typically enforce TLS and at-rest encryption as non-negotiable defaults. DDC's architectural advantage lies in the absence of a single privileged operator, not in encryption guarantees, and documentation or marketing language implying categorical confidentiality superiority should be read with this distinction in mind.

Client-side encryption is a recommended best practice in DDC, not an enforced protocol requirement. The protocol accepts plaintext data uploads without rejection; no encryption is validated or mandated at the infrastructure layer. Developers are strongly encouraged to encrypt data before upload using the SDK's built-in encryption utilities, which support both client-side and server-side encryption workflows. Applications that cannot perform client-side encryption may delegate this operation server-side using the same underlying primitives. In either case, the responsibility for encryption resides with the application layer, not the DDC protocol itself.

Each cluster component receives and retains only encrypted content. Because no cluster component ever receives the DEK, no cluster-side operation, including those performed by Cere Network operators, can produce plaintext from stored data.

Data durability
Your data is distributed across multiple nodes using two strategies:
Small pieces: Replication (multiple copies)
Pieces larger than 16 KB� Erasure coding
If some nodes fail:
Temporary outage: System routes around failures automatically
Permanent loss: Data survives if enough copies/shares remain

Data security

needs.
Access Control:
DDC provides robust access control through bucket permissions
(private/public) and owner-based authentication. This protects your data from
unauthorized external access.
Data Distribution:
DDC uses erasure coding to distribute your data across multiple nodes in the
cluster, providing redundancy and availability. Data is split into fragments, with
each node storing only a portion of your files. For a malicious actor to
reconstruction a file, they require control over a significant portion of the
cluster. Learn more: �ADR� Data redundancy strategy

DDC @Sergey Poluyan 19

DDC's security architecture diverges from centralized cloud providers such as AWS, Azure, Snowflake, and Databricks in one foundational respect: encryption key custody. In those platforms, encryption is typically managed server-side by the provider, meaning plaintext data is accessible to the platform operator under certain access conditions. In DDC, applications are required to encrypt data client-side before submission to storage nodes, ensuring plaintext is never transmitted or stored without prior encryption at the application layer. Combined with on-chain verification of storage activity, this removes the platform operator as a trusted intermediary in the data confidentiality model.

When migrating from managed platforms such as Databricks or Snowflake to DDC, enterprise teams exchange provider-controlled access guarantees for cryptographic ones. Data interactions are attributed to a user-controlled data wallet, and smart contracts govern persistence and access rather than vendor policy. However, teams should evaluate payment continuity carefully: if the application funding data persistence stops paying, the availability of that data is at risk, which raises questions about whether users retain meaningful ownership in practice. Security posture shifts from trusting a managed cloud provider's compliance certifications to trusting on-chain contract execution and node operator behavior.

Context
At the moment DDC uses a simple replication to achieve data redundancy. In order
to achieve desired level of durability, storage makes N copies (e.g. 5). This yields
an expansion factor of 5x, or 500%. Then this duplicated data (x5) needs to be
stored on the network, using bandwidth.
Thus, higher level of durability results in more storage and bandwidth for a fixed
amount of data.
So, the cost of durability is:
higher storage price
higher bandwidth
lower network storage capacity (total size / replication factor)

Erasure coding

As an alternative to simple replication, erasure codes provide a much more
efficient
method to achieve data redundancy.
Erasure codes are an encoding scheme for increasing data durability. This
technique are widely used by both distributed (e.g. AWS S3, Ceph) and peer-to-
peer storage systems (e.g. Storj, mentioned in Filecoin but not clear is it working
or not).
An erasure code is often described by two numbers, K and N. If a block of data is
encoded with a �K, N� erasure code, there are N total generated erasure shares,
where
only any K of them are required to recover the original block of data. If a block of
data
is S bytes, each of the N erasure shares is roughly S/K bytes. Each erasure share
is unique.
More interestingly, erasure codes can increase durability without increasing the
expansion factor. The durability of a �K � 20, N � 40� erasure code is better than a
�K � 10, N =
20� erasure code, even though the expansion factor �2x� is the same for both.
This is because the risk is spread across more nodes in the �K � 20, N � 40� case.

The DDC Resource Manager supports an Active/Standby high availability architecture to eliminate single points of failure in compute scheduling. At any point in time, one Resource Manager instance operates as Active while one or more Standby instances remain ready to assume control if the Active instance becomes unavailable. Failover can be configured to occur automatically, transferring scheduling responsibilities to a Standby instance without manual intervention. Operators should provision at least one Standby Resource Manager in production deployments to ensure continuity of job matching, model lifecycle management, quota enforcement, and SLA adherence during failure events.

[ADR] Encrypted Data Access
and Key Delegation
Status
Proposed by �Sergey Poluyan �Ulad Palinski
Stakeholders: �Fred Jin
Outcome
Status: planned
Executive Summary
This ADR extends the existing DDC authorization system to support encrypted
data storage with delegated key access for AI agents. It builds upon the recursive
token chain model documented in �ADR� Authentication and authorization , adding
encryption primitives that enable secure data sharing between users, data
services, and AI agents.
Key Goals:
Users can upload encrypted data to DDC
Encryption keys can be delegated to data services and AI agents
Access is verifiable and auditable
Keys can be added/revoked for existing data
Same authorization pattern applies to Storage and Compute layers
Context
Concept mapping
[ADR] Encrypted Data Access and Key Delegation 1

Step 7: Encrypt Before Upload

DDC does not encrypt content automatically; protecting data before upload is the responsibility of the client application. Generate a Data Encryption Key (DEK), use it to encrypt your content locally, then upload the resulting ciphertext to DDC. The DEK itself can be managed through the same authorization pattern used across Storage and Compute layers, allowing access to be granted or revoked for existing data without re-uploading content. The following example demonstrates DEK generation, encryption, and upload of ciphertext using the DDC SDK:

const dek = crypto.getRandomValues(new Uint8Array(32));
const encrypted = await encryptContent(plaintext, dek);
await ddcClient.upload(encrypted, { resourceUri });

Step 7: Encrypt Before Upload

DDC does not apply encryption to stored data by default. To protect sensitive content, generate a Data Encryption Key (DEK) on the client side before uploading. Use a standard symmetric cipher such as AES-256-GCM to encrypt the raw bytes locally, then upload the ciphertext to your chosen cluster. Store or escrow the DEK separately from DDC. To retrieve the content, download the ciphertext and decrypt it client-side using the same DEK. Without this step, data written to DDC buckets is stored and served in plaintext.

The data encryption key (DEK) is generated and held exclusively by the client and is never transmitted to or stored by the DDC cluster. Because the cluster retains no copy of the DEK, it cannot decrypt stored content on behalf of any party, including Cere Network operators. No administrative override or cluster-level key recovery mechanism exists. This design ensures that data confidentiality is enforced architecturally rather than by policy, meaning access to plaintext content remains solely under the control of the client that performed the write operation.

DDC stores all uploaded data in plaintext by default. No automatic encryption is applied at the cluster or node level before or after upload. Users who require confidentiality must encrypt data client-side before submitting it to the cluster.

For use cases requiring controlled access to encrypted data, DDC supports an encrypted data access and key delegation model. Access is verifiable and auditable, and keys can be granted or revoked for existing data. This authorization pattern applies uniformly across both the Storage and Compute layers, supporting use cases such as AI agents and other programmatic consumers.

Seed Phrase Handling

When a seed phrase is provided to DdcClient.create(), the SDK derives the corresponding cryptographic key material from it. The seed phrase itself is not persisted to disk or transmitted externally. Security-conscious integrators should treat the seed phrase as sensitive in-process material for the duration of the initialization call and apply standard secure memory practices within their own runtime environment, such as avoiding logging the value and limiting its scope. Once key derivation is complete, retaining the seed phrase in application memory beyond that point is unnecessary and not recommended.

Secure Storage Quick Start

To store encrypted data on DDC, complete the following steps in sequence. First, generate a wallet and fund it with a deposit to cover storage operations. Next, create a bucket to serve as the access-controlled container for your content. Before uploading, apply client-side encryption to your file. Finally, submit the encrypted file to your bucket via the SDK upload method.

Access control is enforced at the node level, meaning only authorized requests are processed by CDN and storage nodes. Read access can be scoped to an entire bucket, a single piece, or a defined collection of pieces.

Your DDC data wallet identity is secured by an sr25519 keypair. Loss of this key results in permanent, unrecoverable loss of access to all associated data. Before storing any data on DDC, record your wallet mnemonic phrase on paper and store copies in at least two physically separate locations. For higher-assurance environments, generate and store the key within a hardware security module or secure key vault, and encrypt any exported key material with a strong passphrase as an additional protection layer. Never store your mnemonic phrase solely in digital form on an internet-connected device.

When initializing a data wallet within the DDC SDK, the output includes a wallet address that serves as the identity anchor for data access and ownership operations. This address is used by DDC's authentication mechanism to associate stored data with a specific owner, enabling access control scenarios such as NFT-gated content. For example, a verified NFT ownership state can be mapped to a wallet address, allowing DDC to grant or revoke data access automatically without requiring direct client involvement in the authorization process.

Before uploading sensitive content, encrypt it client-side using AES-256-GCM prior to calling any DDC storage method. The following pattern is supported with the current SDK: generate a random 256-bit key and IV, encrypt the raw bytes using the Web Crypto API or a compatible Node.js equivalent, then pass the resulting ciphertext buffer to the DDC client upload method. On retrieval, download the ciphertext and decrypt locally using the same key and IV. The DDC client identity uses sr25519 keys; the encryption key is a separate secret managed entirely by the application and never transmitted to the cluster.

DDC's data confidentiality guarantee is entirely dependent on correct client-side implementation. The protocol does not validate or reject unencrypted payloads at ingestion time, meaning that if an application fails to encrypt data before writing to DDC, those records are stored as plaintext across cluster nodes with no automatic remediation. There is no protocol-level enforcement to detect or prevent this condition. Operators and integrators must treat encryption as a mandatory application-layer responsibility and establish independent verification procedures to confirm that all payloads are encrypted prior to submission.

Warning: Permanent Data Loss from Lost Credentials

Access to encrypted data stored in DDC is permanently and irrecoverably lost if the associated seed phrase or Data Encryption Key (DEK) is lost. DDC's decentralized architecture means there is no account recovery mechanism, password reset process, or administrative override available. Users and integrators are solely responsible for securely backing up seed phrases and DEKs using offline or redundant storage methods before writing data to any DDC cluster. Treat these credentials with the same criticality as private keys controlling on-chain assets.

Encryption key delegation in DDC is not currently supported. Access control to encrypted data is managed at the node level, where a client's permissions are determined by their relationship to a given entry point in the data graph. Using the DAG API, data can be structured hierarchically and accessed by path, enabling parent-child node relationships similar to shared folder access. Authorized clients can traverse this structure based on their access to a parent node, but cryptographic key delegation to third parties remains outside the scope of the current implementation.

By default, data uploaded to a DDC bucket is stored as plaintext. Any client with read access to the bucket can retrieve the raw content. To protect sensitive data, encrypt it locally before passing it to the upload method using a Data Encryption Key (DEK). Generate a DEK, encrypt your payload with it, then upload the ciphertext. Access control alone does not substitute for encryption, as authorization governs who may request content but does not obscure the content itself from nodes handling the data in transit or at rest.

By default, DDC stores data as plaintext. To achieve encryption at rest, generate a Data Encryption Key (DEK) on the client before upload, encrypt the content locally using the DEK, and store only the ciphertext in DDC. For per-asset isolation, derive a unique DEK per piece using a nonce and the root DEK via deriveDek(nonce, videoDek) before encrypting with naclDecrypt or its encryption counterpart. Back up the DEK securely outside DDC; loss of the DEK renders encrypted content permanently inaccessible. Server-side encryption is available as a fallback when client-side processing is not possible.

On retrieval, download the ciphertext and decrypt locally using the same key and IV. The DDC client identity uses sr25519 keys; the encryption key is a separate secret managed entirely by the application and never transmitted to the cluster.

Your DDC data wallet identity is secured by an sr25519 keypair. Loss of this key results in permanent, unrecoverable loss of access to all associated data. Before storing any data on DDC, record your wallet mnemonic phrase on paper and store copies in at least two physically separate locations. For higher-assurance environments, generate and store the key within a hardware security module or secure key vault, and encrypt any exported key material with a strong passphrase as an additional protection layer. Never store your mnemonic phrase solely in digital form on an internet-connected device.

By default, the DDC SDK transmits and stores data in plaintext. Encryption is not applied automatically at any layer of the SDK or cluster infrastructure. Enterprise deployments requiring data confidentiality must implement encryption within the application layer before passing data to the SDK.

The recommended approach is AES-256-GCM, a modern symmetric cipher that is computationally resistant to brute-force attacks under current conditions. Encrypt your payload locally, then upload the resulting ciphertext to DDC. Your application must manage key storage and retrieval independently, as the SDK provides no key management facilities in the current release.

When initializing a data wallet within the DDC SDK, the output includes a wallet address that serves as the identity anchor for data access and ownership operations. This address is used by DDC's authentication mechanism to associate stored data with a specific owner, enabling access control scenarios such as NFT-gated content. For example, a verified NFT ownership state can be mapped to a wallet address, allowing DDC to grant or revoke data access automatically without requiring direct client involvement in the authorization process.

Secure Storage Quick Start

To store encrypted data on DDC, complete the following steps in sequence. First, generate a wallet and fund it with a deposit to cover storage operations. Next, create a bucket to serve as the access-controlled container for your content. Before uploading, apply client-side encryption to your file. Finally, submit the encrypted file to your bucket via the SDK upload method.

Access control is enforced at the node level, meaning only authorized requests are processed by CDN and storage nodes. Read access can be scoped to an entire bucket, a single piece, or a defined collection of pieces.

Seed Phrase Handling

When a seed phrase is provided to DdcClient.create(), the SDK derives the corresponding cryptographic key material from it. The seed phrase itself is not persisted to disk or transmitted externally. Security-conscious integrators should treat the seed phrase as sensitive in-process material for the duration of the initialization call and apply standard secure memory practices within their own runtime environment, such as avoiding logging the value and limiting its scope. Once key derivation is complete, retaining the seed phrase in application memory beyond that point is unnecessary and not recommended.

DDC does not currently enforce TLS on client-to-node connections by default. Data is transmitted in plaintext between clients and storage nodes, and production deployments should confirm that TLS is enforced independently of any SDK-level encryption controls. Operators running storage nodes or CDN nodes within a cluster are responsible for validating that transport layer security is applied at the infrastructure level. Applications handling sensitive data should not rely solely on DDC defaults and must independently verify that encrypted transport is configured before moving workloads to production.

The SDK authenticates all operations by signing requests with a user key derived from a seed or wallet. Blockchain transactions are signed and sent to the chain endpoint, while storage and compute requests to DDC nodes are signed by the same identity and verified at the node level. This unified signing model ensures that a single user identity governs both on-chain and off-chain interactions, eliminating the need for separate credential management across the decentralized infrastructure.

When a bucket is successfully created on DDC, the submitting wallet address is automatically assigned as the bucket owner. The bucket owner holds root access to that bucket, meaning they retain full administrative control over its contents and access policies. This ownership is derived deterministically from the wallet address used to sign the creation transaction. Protecting the private key associated with the owner wallet is therefore critical, as compromise of that key constitutes full compromise of the bucket and any data stored within it.

When a bucket is created in DDC, the owner is automatically granted root access to that bucket. This root access allows the owner to perform all operations on the bucket, including read, write, and delete, as well as delegate access permissions to other parties. The owner can grant scoped access to additional users, restricting or extending their permissions as needed. For example, a bucket owner may share access with multiple users, enabling each to upload or download data while the owner retains ultimate control over the bucket's access policies.

When a bucket is created in DDC, the wallet address that initiated the transaction automatically becomes the bucket owner. Bucket ownership confers root access, representing the highest level of permission within that bucket's access control hierarchy. This ownership model is enforced at the pallet level, providing a foundational layer of access governance. From this privileged position, the bucket owner can subsequently grant access to other parties, enabling delegated permission structures while retaining ultimate control over the bucket and its contents.

The Data Activity Capture (DAC) layer functions as the trust foundation within DDC, monitoring real-time traffic and compute metrics across clusters. These verified measurements are recorded as validation results on the Cere Blockchain, which serves as an immutable repository ensuring transparency in data operations. By anchoring activity metrics to on-chain validation, DAC enables automated, auditable payouts without reliance on a single service provider. This architecture ensures that all billing and access decisions are grounded in cryptographically verifiable data rather than centralized reporting, preserving the integrity of the broader DDC trust model.

All access decisions in DDC are grounded in cryptographically verifiable data rather than centralized reporting. By anchoring activity metrics to on-chain validation, the Data Availability Committee (DAC) enables automated, auditable evaluation of both billing and access requests without reliance on a single service provider. This architecture preserves the integrity of the broader DDC trust model, ensuring that no unilateral or opaque authority can govern data operations. The result is a transparent, tamper-resistant foundation in which access control outcomes can be independently verified by any participant in the network.

This architecture ensures that automated payouts and access decisions are grounded in cryptographically verifiable activity rather than self-reported data. Each interaction is anchored to a data wallet keypair, which underpins the trust model governing all data operations across DDC clusters.

When a bucket is created on DDC, the wallet that initiated the creation is designated as the bucket owner and is automatically granted root access to that bucket. This ownership model is enforced at the pallet level, providing high-level access control as a foundational layer of the DDC authentication and authorization architecture. Following creation, the bucket owner may grant access to other clients, enabling delegated permissions without transferring ownership. This combined approach integrates pallet-based access control with token-based access delegation to support flexible, multi-party data access scenarios.

The DDC authentication gateway relies on bucket ownership and access control records anchored at the blockchain pallet level. Because this ownership model is enforced on-chain, the gateway depends on the Cere Blockchain as its authoritative source for client permissions. When the blockchain is unavailable and no cached records exist for a given client, the gateway cannot independently verify ownership or delegated access rights, and authentication requests for that client will not succeed until connectivity is restored or a valid cache entry becomes available.

Bucket access permissions granted through the pallet-based access control layer are revocable by the bucket owner at any time. Following initial bucket creation, the owner may grant delegated permissions to other clients without transferring ownership, and may subsequently revoke those permissions as access requirements change. This revocability is a deliberate property of the foundational authentication and authorization architecture, ensuring that multi-party data access scenarios remain under the continuous control of the originating owner. Token-based access delegation operates alongside pallet-based controls to support flexible permission management across the full access lifecycle.

The authentication gater maintains bucket and customer indexes that are persisted on disk, enabling efficient lookup and validation of access rights without requiring repeated on-chain queries. These indexes are loaded at node startup and updated as new buckets are created or customer permissions change, ensuring the gater operates with current state while minimizing latency during request handling.

By storing these indexes locally, DDC nodes can authenticate incoming requests against bucket ownership and customer entitlements in a performant manner. This approach supports the broader DDC security model, where smart contracts define permissions and node-level components enforce them at the data access layer.

By storing this index data locally, DDC nodes can enforce access policies at the edge of the cluster while remaining synchronized with the authoritative state recorded on-chain. This design supports the trustless data transfer model central to DDC, where security guarantees are preserved without sacrificing throughput.

Generate a 256-bit data encryption key using crypto.getRandomValues, then encrypt the payload with AES-256-GCM before passing the resulting ciphertext as the upload body. const dek = crypto.getRandomValues(new Uint8Array(32));
const iv = crypto.getRandomValues(new Uint8Array(12));
const key = await crypto.subtle.importKey("raw", dek, "AES-GCM", false, ["encrypt"]);
const ciphertext = await crypto.subtle.encrypt({ name: "AES-GCM", iv }, key, plaintext);
await client.store(bucketId, new Uint8Array(ciphertext));

The data encryption key (DEK) is generated and held exclusively by the client and is never transmitted to or stored by the DDC cluster. Clients are responsible for retaining the corresponding DEK through external key management mechanisms of their choosing. Because the cluster retains no copy of the DEK, it cannot decrypt stored content on behalf of any party, including Cere Network operators. No administrative override or cluster-level key recovery mechanism exists; loss of the DEK by the client results in permanent inaccessibility of the associated encrypted data.

By default, data is transmitted in plaintext between clients and storage nodes, and TLS is not enforced on client-to-node connections. The architectural claim that DDC removes the platform operator as a trusted intermediary in the confidentiality model is only realized when client-side encryption is explicitly implemented by the application developer. Without these measures in place, DDC provides no confidentiality guarantees beyond those of a conventional centralized storage system.
